annotations.label: Curve (informal)

anchor.snippet: a curve (also called a curved line in older texts) is an object similar to a line

annotations.note: Mathlib has no single informal/general notion of 'curve'; only specific incarnations such as Path, IsIntegralCurve, and WeierstrassCurve.

annotations.label: Curve as image of an interval

anchor.snippet: A curve is the image of an interval to a topological space by a continuous function

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Path` is a continuous map `C(I, X)` from the unit interval, the standard incarnation of this informal definition.

annotations.label: Parametrization / parametric curve

anchor.snippet: the function that defines the curve is called a parametrization , and the curve is a parametric curve

annotations.note: Mathlib treats the parametrizing function itself (as in `Path` or generic `γ : ℝ → E`) rather than naming a parametrization concept separately.

annotations.label: Differentiable curve (lead)

anchor.snippet: the function that defines a curve is often supposed to be differentiable , and the curve is then said to be a differentiable curve

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Differentiable`/`ContDiff` for arbitrary maps but no dedicated 'differentiable curve' structure.

annotations.label: Plane algebraic curve (lead)

anchor.snippet: A plane algebraic curve is the zero set of a polynomial in two indeterminates

annotations.note: No general definition of plane algebraic curve; only special families like `WeierstrassCurve` are formalized.

annotations.label: Algebraic curve (lead)

anchor.snippet: an algebraic curve is the zero set of a finite set of polynomials, which satisfies the further condition of being an algebraic variety of dimension one

annotations.note: Mathlib has no generic `AlgebraicCurve` notion as a one-dimensional variety.

annotations.label: Curve defined over a field

anchor.snippet: If the coefficients of the polynomials belong to a field

annotations.note: There is no general 'curve defined over F' concept; only specific structures like `WeierstrassCurve R` are parametrized by their base ring.

annotations.label: Real algebraic curve (lead)

anchor.snippet: In the common case of a real algebraic curve

annotations.note: No dedicated 'real algebraic curve' definition was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Complex algebraic curve / Riemann surface (lead)

anchor.snippet: When complex zeros are considered, one has a complex algebraic curve

annotations.note: No formalization of complex algebraic curves or Riemann surfaces was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Topological curve

anchor.snippet: A topological curve can be specified by a continuous function

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Path` (continuous `I → X`) is essentially a topological curve on the unit interval; no general 'topological curve' name.

annotations.label: Peano / space-filling curve

anchor.snippet: the image of the Peano curve or, more generally, a space-filling curve completely fills a square

annotations.note: A Peano-curve construction lives in `Counterexamples/PeanoCurve.lean`, but nothing inside `Mathlib/` formalizes space-filling curves.

annotations.label: Closed curve / loop

anchor.snippet: is closed [ b ] or is a loop if

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Path` does not have a dedicated 'closed path/loop' subtype; loops appear implicitly via `Path x x`.

annotations.label: Path / arc

anchor.snippet: the curve is called a path , also known as topological arc

annotations.note: `Path x y` is defined as a continuous map from `I` with endpoints `x` and `y`, matching the topological notion of a path.

annotations.label: Simple curve

anchor.snippet: A curve is simple if it is the image of an interval or a circle by an injective continuous function

annotations.note: No definition of 'simple curve' (injective parametrization) was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Plane / space / skew curve

anchor.snippet: A plane curve is a curve for which

annotations.note: No notions of plane/space/skew curves as distinguished classes were found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Jordan curve

anchor.snippet: A plane simple closed curve is also called a Jordan curve

annotations.note: Grepping for `JordanCurve` returned no hits in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Jordan curve theorem

anchor.snippet: The Jordan curve theorem states that the set complement in a plane of a Jordan curve consists of two connected components

annotations.note: The Jordan curve theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Jordan domain

anchor.snippet: The bounded region inside a Jordan curve is known as Jordan domain

annotations.note: No definition of Jordan domain is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fractal curve with positive area

anchor.snippet: a fractal curve can have a Hausdorff dimension bigger than one

annotations.note: Hausdorff dimension exists in Mathlib but no fractal-curve examples are formalized.

annotations.label: Differentiable curve

anchor.snippet: Roughly speaking a differentiable curve is a curve that is defined as being locally the image of an injective differentiable function

annotations.note: No structure capturing 'differentiable curve as a subset locally diffeomorphic to an interval' exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Differentiable curve (precise)

anchor.snippet: a differentiable curve is a subset

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalization of differentiable curves as subsets locally parametrized by injective differentiable maps.

annotations.label: Arc (Euclidean geometry)

anchor.snippet: an arc (symbol: ⌒ ) is a connected subset of a differentiable curve

annotations.note: No notion of 'arc' as a connected subset of a differentiable curve was found.

annotations.label: Segments, rays, lines

anchor.snippet: Arcs of lines are called segments , rays , or lines

annotations.note: Mathlib has `segment` and `openSegment` in vector spaces, but no specific 'ray' or 'line as arc' definitions linked to the curve framework.

annotations.label: Circular arc

anchor.snippet: A common curved example is an arc of a circle

annotations.note: No dedicated definition of circular arc was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Great arc

anchor.snippet: an arc of a great circle (or a great ellipse ) is called a great arc

annotations.note: Great arcs of great circles or ellipses are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Length of a curve (Euclidean)

anchor.snippet: then the length of

annotations.note: `eVariationOn` is the total-variation length applicable to Euclidean curves; no specific integral-of-norm-derivative formula is packaged as 'curve length'.

annotations.label: Length independent of parametrization

anchor.snippet: The length of a curve is independent of the parametrization

annotations.note: Reparametrization-invariance of `eVariationOn` under monotone bijections expresses length independence from parametrization.

annotations.label: Length of a graph

anchor.snippet: In particular, the length

annotations.note: No specific formula for the arclength of the graph of a function `y = f(x)` was found.

annotations.label: Length of a curve in a metric space

anchor.snippet: then we can define the length of a curve

annotations.note: `eVariationOn f s` is defined as the supremum of `Σ edist (f (u (i+1))) (f (u i))` and is the metric-space length/total-variation of a curve.

annotations.label: Rectifiable curve

anchor.snippet: A rectifiable curve is a curve with finite length

annotations.note: `BoundedVariationOn f s := eVariationOn f s ≠ ∞` matches the finite-length/rectifiable condition.

annotations.label: Natural / unit-speed curve

anchor.snippet: is called natural (or unit-speed or parametrized by arc length)

annotations.note: `HasUnitSpeedOn f s` states the speed of `f` on `s` is `1`, i.e., unit-speed/arc-length parametrization.

annotations.label: Lipschitz curves are rectifiable

anchor.snippet: is a Lipschitz-continuous function, then it is automatically rectifiable

annotations.note: `LipschitzWith.locallyBoundedVariationOn` shows any Lipschitz function on `ℝ` has locally bounded variation, the rectifiability statement.

annotations.label: Metric derivative / speed

anchor.snippet: one can define the speed (or metric derivative ) of

annotations.note: Mathlib has `HasConstantSpeedOnWith`/`HasUnitSpeedOn` capturing the value of metric speed, but no standalone definition of the metric-derivative function.

annotations.label: World line in general relativity

anchor.snippet: In general relativity , a world line is a curve in spacetime

annotations.note: General relativity / worldlines are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Smooth curve in a smooth manifold

anchor.snippet: a smooth curve in

annotations.note: Smooth curves are instances of `ContMDiff` maps from `ℝ` into a smooth manifold; no dedicated 'smooth curve' wrapper exists.

annotations.label: C^k curve and analytic curve

anchor.snippet: There are less and more restricted ideas, too

annotations.note: `ContDiff`/`AnalyticOn` apply to curves but no notion specialized to one-variable parametrizations exists.

annotations.label: Regular differentiable curve

anchor.snippet: A differentiable curve is said to be regular if its derivative never vanishes

annotations.note: No definition of 'regular curve' (nowhere-vanishing derivative) was found.

annotations.label: Equivalent curves / reparametrization / arc

anchor.snippet: are said to be equivalent if there is a bijective

annotations.note: No equivalence relation on differentiable curves under reparametrization is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Plane algebraic curve

anchor.snippet: A plane algebraic curve is the set of the points of coordinates

annotations.note: Mathlib has no general definition of a plane algebraic curve as the zero locus of a bivariate polynomial.

annotations.label: Curve defined over F

anchor.snippet: the curve is said to be defined over

annotations.note: No generic 'curve defined over a field' definition exists; only the base ring of specific structures like `WeierstrassCurve R`.

annotations.label: Real point and real part

anchor.snippet: a point with real coordinates is a real point , and the set of all real points is the real part of the curve

annotations.note: No formalization of real points / real part of an algebraic curve.

annotations.label: Riemann surface (algebraic)

anchor.snippet: the nonsingular complex projective algebraic curves are called Riemann surfaces

annotations.note: Riemann surfaces are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rational points on a curve

anchor.snippet: are said to be rational over

annotations.note: For Weierstrass curves, `Affine.Point` gives F-rational points, but no general 'rational points' for arbitrary curves.

annotations.label: Fermat's Last Theorem as rational points

anchor.snippet: Fermat's Last Theorem may be restated as

annotations.note: `FermatLastTheorem` is stated arithmetically over `ℕ`, not as rational points on the Fermat curve.

annotations.label: Algebraic space curves / complete intersection

anchor.snippet: Algebraic curves can also be space curves

annotations.note: No general space-curve or complete-intersection-curve definitions found.

annotations.label: Projective completion of a plane curve

anchor.snippet: A plane curve may also be completed to a curve in the projective plane

annotations.note: No generic projective-completion construction for plane curves; only Weierstrass curves have projective coordinates.

annotations.label: Fermat curve

anchor.snippet: An example is the Fermat curve

annotations.note: The Fermat curve as a geometric object is not formalized; only the arithmetic statement `FermatLastTheorem` exists.

annotations.label: Conics

anchor.snippet: the simplest examples of algebraic curves are the conics , which are nonsingular curves of degree two and genus zero

annotations.note: No definition of conic sections as algebraic curves of degree two was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Elliptic curves

anchor.snippet: Elliptic curves , which are nonsingular curves of genus one

annotations.note: Elliptic curves are formalized as a `WeierstrassCurve` with the `IsElliptic` typeclass (invertible discriminant).
